Actually, this is one of the big change I made to the Link and Chain classes. Now `get_transfomration_matrix` can have 0, 1 or several parameters (as said in the previous PR, which I closed). I think changing the Link interface is not harmful for backward compatibility. But now you can't count the number of parameters of a Chain by counting the number of links. I removed this check to make the function more generous in what it accepts. I changed the comments to reflect the change. My thinking is that the purpose of compression is to ensure that there are no more than the target number of bytes, which is satisfied if target > length. So now if target > length, the original bytes are returned since there is no compression needed. 

I do understand that this is a different behavior. The impact on end users is that hashing small inputs will not cause an error, which I think is more intuitive. Another solution would be to pad input bytes up to the target number, but this would lead to padded final outputs.

For example:
```python
digest = '010203'

# Old method
humanhash.humanize(digest, 6)
Traceback (most recent call last):
...
ValueError: Fewer input bytes than requested output

# New method
humanhash.humanize(digest, 6)
'alabama-alanine-alaska'

# Padding method
humanhash.humanize(digest, 6)
'alabama-alanine-alaska-ack-ack-ack'
```

Thanks for your explanations. I still think that it must be formalized somewhere "officially" but this is the idea.

By the way, I find this format language very uncomfortable and unintuitive, but I guess it cannot be helped and this is the best that could have been done. Also this would be so much easier to use **if this was documented somewhere**. People can't think to use stuff it they are not documented. I don't know how long ago this feature exists (I don't remind such a thing from back in the days) and how long ago the `color` field method has been deprecated, but **it's urgent and capital to write something about it in the official documentation**. Else casual contributors like me will continue coding with their feet and everybody will spend more time on debugging stupidities, which leads to less time to important thing like documentation and "good" coding, which is a vicious circle.

I know you're also a (much less) casual contributor (than me) so that's why it needs to be formalized and acknowledged by an official core developer.

I'm not mad, I just express my feeling about what I objectively think to be the good way to progress, so please excuse my strong sentences with **bold text**, if it offend someone that surely wasn't my intention.

>adding multiple formats

Module enhancement. Uptime is so boring if it can't do anything else. This now display uptime in few different formats. I skipped `since` format because it's too different, but will leave it to others if they want to include this format. I don't.

>obscure placeholder names is not the way to go imho.

I agree, but this is timedate. I feel we should make an exception for this. See `pomodoro` for an example. Pomodoro uses `{ss}`, `{mm}`, etc. I went with simple variables similar to `man date` for placeholders. All of them except for `{G}` to represent decades.

If we spell it out, then the format gets awful too long, then I made multiple `format_minutes` things... then we say we want everything in one format instead, but with full datetime names in one format... along with the formatter... it gets awfully too long.

If I go back to `format_minutes` thing, then this will be similar to upcoming `weather_owm` module where they allows custom formats for each `{weather}` thing.

I think, in a nutshell, that it's best we make an exception for timedate so we can put everything in one format (including the formatter) like what I did here. The multiple formats are the module enhancement that I'd like to add.

I think line 104 could be extracted as separate method for readability, such as:
```python
def _convert_to_unix_path(path):
    return path.replace('\\', '/')
```

Then it could be used at the beginning of `_save`, like:
```python
def _save(self, name, content):
    name = self._convert_to_unix_path(name)
    name = self._prepend_prefix(name)
    content = UploadedFile(content, name)
    response = self._upload(name, content)
    return response['public_id']
```

Apart from readability, it would be a little safer as replacing backslashes with slashes at the end of `_prepend_prefix` is dangerous as for example `_normalize_path` would append unnecessary `/` to windows style path due to `if path != '' and not path.endswith('/')` condition.

I realized that we need to change the order of the DMLC_DECLARE_FIELD here. In the original version, the parameters that do not have default values will be set first and then goes the params with default values. So the order will be `kernel`, `pool_type`, `global_pool`, `cudnn_off`, ... After we add default values to `kernel` and `pool_type`, the order becomes `global_pool`, `cudnn_off`, `kernel`, `pool_type`, ... Thus, the way to solve the problem is to change the order:
```c++
    DMLC_DECLARE_FIELD(kernel).set_default(TShape())  // add default value here
    .enforce_nonzero()
    .describe("Pooling kernel size: (y, x) or (d, y, x)");

    DMLC_DECLARE_FIELD(pool_type).set_default(pool_enum::kMaxPooling)  // add default pooling method
    .add_enum("max", pool_enum::kMaxPooling)
    .add_enum("avg", pool_enum::kAvgPooling)
    .add_enum("sum", pool_enum::kSumPooling)
    .describe("Pooling type to be applied.");

    DMLC_DECLARE_FIELD(global_pool).set_default(false)
    .describe("Ignore kernel size, do global pooling based on current input feature map. ");

    DMLC_DECLARE_FIELD(cudnn_off).set_default(false)
    .describe("Turn off cudnn pooling and use MXNet pooling operator. ");
```

Let's add some description that div_scalar only operates on input.data if input is sparse. 
For example, if input of shape (100 x 100) has only 2 nnz, i.e. input.data = [5,6], scalar = nan, it will result output.data = [nan, nan] instead of 10000 nans. 

The following is the scipy behavior: 

```
>>> import scipy.sparse as spsp
>>> csr = spsp.rand(5,5,format='csr', density=0.5)
>>> csr
<5x5 sparse matrix of type '<type 'numpy.float64'>'
	with 12 stored elements in Compressed Sparse Row format>
>>> csr / np.nan
<5x5 sparse matrix of type '<type 'numpy.float64'>'
	with 12 stored elements in Compressed Sparse Row format>
>>> csr.todense() / np.nan
matrix([[ nan,  nan,  nan,  nan,  nan],
        [ nan,  nan,  nan,  nan,  nan],
        [ nan,  nan,  nan,  nan,  nan],
        [ nan,  nan,  nan,  nan,  nan],
        [ nan,  nan,  nan,  nan,  nan]])
```

I think your best bet to track this down would be to test it and see what error message you get.  But this function retriggers jobs, and it's best not to do that unless you know it's needed.  So you can cut off the action in this function by adding a return on line 276, or thereabouts.
I would also re-add the old function to make the ``const`` of ``builderToTaskOld`` or something.  Then after your new function, print both the old and new to the console to be sure you're getting the same result.

|                                                       | ciso8601 v1.0.7     | ciso8601 v2.0.0 (Python 3.6) | % Change |
| ----------------------------------------------------- | ------------------- | ---------------------------- | -------- |
| `parse_datetime(u'2014-01-09T21:48:00.921000')`       | 0.180               | 0.181                        | 0.55555  |

### Better Performance measurement

Unfortunately, not having a accurate benchmarking script was hampering my progress. For example, using timeit in two separate calls as I did in my previous comment gave the impression that things were 12% slower. But if I rerun the test, I get drastically different results each time.

This is the most stable benchmarking code I have thus far. I'm mostly happy with it:

```
import random
import sys

import ciso8601
import ciso8601_v2

functions = [
    ciso8601.parse_datetime,
    ciso8601.parse_datetime_unaware,
    ciso8601_v2.parse_datetime,
    ciso8601_v2.parse_datetime_as_naive
]

INPUT_TIMESTAMP = sys.argv[1]
for i in range(1_000_000):
    random.shuffle(functions)
    for function in functions:
        try:
            function(INPUT_TIMESTAMP)
        except ValueError:
            print(str(function))
            raise
```

I then run it using cProfile:

`python -m cProfile -o test.profile performance_test.py`

And manually copy values out of the profile file. It's manually intensive, but gives consistent results. I'll have to look into either better tools, or further automate this one.

    Traceback (most recent call last):

    AttributeError: 'RpcError' object has no attribute 'details'

    it doesn't (on it's own) have the information we want to extract from a caught exception

A comment about py2 and 3 compatible code. I guess this was developed on Py3 as you use print as a function. However, the `print()` only work like it is supposed to in Py2, if there is only one argument. If doing `print("more", "strings")` it will print out `("more", "strings")`. The proper way to do it is to start the file with: `from __future__ import print_function`. Then print will actually be a function also in Py2.

While you are at it, you might as well also add `from __future__ import print_function, unicode_literals`. Then you will not need to preface your strings with u to make sure they are unicode.

> This is asserting that you can get the representation and just paste it in a python shell for example?

Yes. That is exactly correct.

> Also I'm interested more on the reason to do this kind of assertion :smile:

The job of both `__repr__` and `__str__` is to provide a string representation of an object. However, they provide different kinds of string representations. From the docs:

> In short, the goal of `__repr__` is to be unambiguous and `__str__` is to be readable.

`__repr__` should be *so* unambiguous that you can copy-and-paste its output in to a shell and get the same object back. From the docs for [`__repr__`](https://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#object.__repr__):

> If at all possible, this should look like a valid Python expression that could be used to recreate an object with the same value (given an appropriate environment).

Here's another example of `__repr__` and `__str__`:

```python
>>> import datetime
>>> today = datetime.datetime.now()
>>> str(today)
'2012-03-14 09:21:58.130922'
>>> repr(today)
'datetime.datetime(2012, 3, 14, 9, 21, 58, 130922)'
```

As you can see, `__repr__` provides an "official" representation of an object that can be used to recreate a new object with the same value.

I've decided to implement `__repr__` instead of `_-str__` because the former is usable in a wider variety of situations and because it will be more useful for debugging. Check this out:

one important distinction between `__str__` and `__repr__` is what happens when a container is stringified. Check this out:

```python
>>> class Foo(object):
...   def __str__(self):
...     return 'Yellow Submarine'
... 
>>> str(Foo())
'Yellow Submarine'
>>> repr(Foo())
'<__main__.Foo object at 0x7f9ba3f86438>'
>>> str([Foo(), Foo()])
'[<__main__.Foo object at 0x7f9ba3f86438>, <__main__.Foo object at 0x7f9ba3f864a8>]'
```

What happens above is that Python calls `__repr__` on all of the elements of a container even when the container itself defines `__str__`.

I don't think we need all this complexity. Backward compatibility can be achieved just by aliasing classes if we ever move them. 

For example, with `apps.basket.forms.BasketLineFormSet` you would just put this into `forms.py`:

```
# Load the new class from the correct location
MovedBasketLineFormSet = get_class('basket.formsets', 'BasketLineFormSet')

# Subclass the new class into the old one, so that the old one can still be loaded.
@deprecated
class BasketLineFormSet(MovedBasketLineFormSet):
    pass
```

Then you just delete this deprecated class when the deprecation period has ended.

I think I'm partially being biased by the parser, where my hope was that downstream users would subclass `parser` to allow customization of behavior, but even in the parser, I'm thinking that might not be the right design for this particular sort of task.

After giving it a bit of thought, I believe that since ISO-8601 has a very clear set of options it supports, it might be best if it we refactored this to be more like `parser`/`parserinfo`, where `isoparserinfo` would have some limited feature flags like `ordinal_dates` and `weeks` (so that there's not this artificial division between "common" and "uncommon").

In PROV datamodel the [role](https://www.w3.org/TR/2013/REC-prov-dm-20130430/#term-attribute-role) is not typed, but it is a [prov:Role](https://www.w3.org/TR/2013/REC-prov-o-20130430/#hadRole) in PROV-O.  In either case, we should declare it as part of the prospective provenance so that the link to the step is explicit and not just hidden in the URI.

just to make this clearer for others, say you have a transaction, `tx` you wish to consume:

```python
cid = 0
condition = tx['transaction']['conditions'][cid]
input_ = {
    'fulfillment': condition['condition']['details'],
    'input': {
        'cid': cid,
        'txid': tx['id'],
    },
    'owners_before': condition['owners_after'],
}
```
